danger_signals.visual_form: Large, visible enemy sprites placed on dungeon field screens
danger_signals.meaning: Player can see and choose to engage danger; larger-than-player visual scale indicates threat magnitude

danger_signals.signal: Gray arrow path blockage on world map
danger_signals.visual_form: Grayed-out directional indicators on linear travel routes
danger_signals.meaning: Impassable territory; danger or incompletion of prerequisites blocks forward progression

danger_signals.visual_form: Shift from exploration top-down view to dedicated battle window interface
danger_signals.meaning: Player has engaged with hostile entity; combat state is now active

danger_signals.visual_form: Enemies rendered significantly larger than player character on battle screen despite camera distance
danger_signals.meaning: Visual intimidation and threat assessment; scale disparity communicates power/danger level

safety_signals.visual_form: Pictorial settlement representations accessible via open paths
safety_signals.meaning: Safe haven locations; NPCs and merchant vendors present; restoration and story progression occur here

safety_signals.visual_form: Clear directional indicators on world map connecting accessible locations
safety_signals.meaning: Safe travel corridor; player has cleared prerequisites; progression is available

safety_signals.visual_form: No mandatory save points; manual save access at any time in field screen
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and safety; world-state can be preserved at player discretion without resource limitation

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Large enemy sprites representing corrupted elemental servants
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Sealed dungeons with element-themed challenges; barred paths until crystals reclaimed
Allied NPC presence in towns; progression through dungeon completion unlocks new paths

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gray arrow blockage on world map; sealed dungeon entrance with locked doors; barred pathways
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Stationary enemies occupy dungeon space; NPCs express concern in dialogue; world map remains constrained

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gray arrow becomes active (white/normal); dungeon entrances open; new world map paths become traversable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy sprites vanish from cleared dungeons; NPCs provide gratitude dialogue; player gains large EXP/GP rewards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Battlefield icon appears on world map; battle screen loads immediately upon entry
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must defeat ten consecutive enemy encounters to 'clean' the battlefield; each enemy encounter fully resolved before next begins

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Battlefield icon remains but enemy encounters cease; large reward container appears
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No further combat triggers; player receives armor piece, magic spell, large GP, or large EXP deposit

environmental_storytelling.technique: Linear path gating with visual blockage
environmental_storytelling.example: Gray arrows indicate locked routes on world map; player understands dungeon completion is prerequisite before seeing detailed story explanation
Towns, dungeons, and battlefields represented as discrete pictorial icons; player learns world structure through icon exploration rather than continuous scrolling

environmental_storytelling.technique: Enemy sprite placement as danger narrative
environmental_storytelling.example: Stationary enemy sprites visible in dungeon field communicate 'this space is contested'; their removal signals player victory and dungeon safety restoration

environmental_storytelling.technique: Puzzle elements tied to exploration tools
environmental_storytelling.example: Axe for tree-chopping, bombs for sealed doors, grappling hook for gaps — tool necessity communicates environmental narrative (blockage type implies solution)
Ten-battle requirement to 'clean' a battlefield visually demonstrates player pacification of a dangerous region; reward variation (armor/spell/GP/EXP) signals world-state change

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Stationary sprite; player can choose avoidance or engagement; no forced random encounters
Conditional turn-based combat; largest enemies may use crushing/physical attacks; can be defeated, fled from, or survived
Top-down navigation; can interact with objects/NPCs; uses equipped tools (axe, bombs, grappling hook) for environmental manipulation
Game Over triggered; player offered continue option, but character attack power temporarily penalized as narrative/mechanical consequence
Dialogue expresses concern/urgency; may indicate blocked paths; communicate dungeon objectives
Dialogue shifts to gratitude; world-state progression acknowledged; may open new dialogue branches or quest pathways

notes: Final Fantasy Mystic Quest uses strong visual gating (gray arrows, locked doors, sealed dungeons) to communicate world-state without reliance on text. The elimination of random encounters in favor of stationary, visible enemies allows players to visually assess danger and choose engagement. Enemy sprite scaling (larger than player) provides immediate threat assessment. The crystal-recovery mechanic creates binary world states (blocked/open) that are clearly manifested through map visuals. This design favors clarity and accessibility — a stated design goal for 'entry-level players.' Environmental storytelling relies heavily on tool-puzzle correspondence (tool type implies environmental challenge type) rather than detailed narrative exposition. The save-anywhere system removes resource scarcity and communicates safety/player agency during exploration.
